metadata.title: Inflation and Unemployment
metadata.subtitle: Understanding Economic Indicators That Affect Your Life

content.title: Inflation and Unemployment
content.subtitle: Understanding Economic Indicators That Affect Your Life

content.label: Your Parents' Dollar vs. Your Dollar
content.question:
In 1970, minimum wage was $1.60/hour and could buy a McDonald's meal for $0.50, gas for $0.36/gallon, and a movie ticket for $1.55.

In 2024, minimum wage is $7.25/hour but a McDonald's meal costs $10, gas is $3.50/gallon, and a movie ticket is $12.

Key Problem: Prices rose FASTER than wages. Your dollar buys 40% LESS than your parents' dollar did!

Today's goal: Understand inflation, unemployment, and how they affect YOUR purchasing power and job prospects.

objectives.description: inflation and unemployment and explain how they're measured (CPI, unemployment rate)
the three causes of inflation and three types of unemployment
the Phillips Curve trade-off and why stagflation is dangerous
how inflation and unemployment directly affect your budget, job prospects, and life decisions

terms.definition: Sustained increase in the general price level of goods and services—your dollar buys LESS over time
terms.example: If inflation is 3%, a $100 item costs $103 next year

terms.definition: Measures average change in prices paid by urban consumers for a market basket of goods and services
terms.example: CPI tracks 80,000+ items monthly: food, housing, gas, healthcare, etc.

terms.definition: The amount of goods and services your money can buy—decreases with inflation
terms.example: With 5% inflation, $10,000 loses $500 of purchasing power in one year

terms.definition: Percentage of labor force actively seeking work but unable to find jobs
All people age 16+ who are employed OR actively seeking employment
terms.example: Excludes students, retirees, stay-at-home parents not seeking work

terms.definition: Historical relationship showing inflation and unemployment move in opposite directions—when one rises, the other tends to fall
terms.example: Lower unemployment → Higher wages → Higher inflation

leftColumn.title: Demand-Pull Inflation
items: Too much money chasing too few goods
Example: 2021-2022 stimulus checks + supply chain issues → 9% inflation

rightColumn.title: Cost-Push Inflation
items: Supply decreases while demand stays constant
Example: Oil crisis of 1973 → Gas prices tripled → Everything got more expensive

layoutData.title: The Federal Reserve and Money Supply
paragraphs: When the Federal Reserve prints too much money or keeps interest rates too low for too long, the money supply grows faster than the economy.
More dollars in circulation means each dollar is worth less—prices rise to reflect this.
Historical Example: Weimar Germany (1920s) printed massive amounts of money to pay WWI debts. Result: Prices doubled every few days. People needed wheelbarrows of cash to buy bread!
bulletPoints: Result: Inflation peaked at 9.1% in June 2022
layoutData.keyPoint: Money supply growth must match economic growth—too much money = inflation

leftColumn.title: Frictional Unemployment
items: Example: Recent college grad searching for first job

layoutData.title: Unemployment from Economic Downturns
paragraphs: Cyclical unemployment rises during recessions when overall demand for goods and services falls. Businesses lay off workers because consumers aren't buying.
This is the WORST type of unemployment because it affects millions simultaneously and can last years.
2008 Financial Crisis Example: Unemployment rose from 5% (2007) to 10% (2009). 8.7 million jobs lost. Many workers unemployed for over a year.
bulletPoints: Government response: Stimulus spending, unemployment benefits, job programs
Federal Reserve response: Lower interest rates to stimulate borrowing
Recovery can take years—unemployment didn't return to pre-crisis levels until 2015
layoutData.keyPoint: Cyclical unemployment requires aggressive government and Fed action to reduce economic damage

leftPanel.title: What Is the Natural Rate?
paragraphs: The natural rate is the lowest unemployment rate consistent with stable inflation—typically 4-5%.
It includes frictional + structural unemployment but NOT cyclical.
Even in a healthy economy, some unemployment is normal and expected.
Why It Matters: If unemployment falls BELOW the natural rate, inflation tends to accelerate.

highlightBox.text: Zero unemployment is impossible AND undesirable—it would signal extreme labor shortages and wage-driven inflation

infoCard.title: {{STATE_NAME}} Context
items: Compare to national average to understand your local job market

layoutData.title: Inflation vs. Unemployment: The Historical Relationship
paragraphs: The Phillips Curve shows a historical inverse relationship: when unemployment falls, inflation tends to rise—and vice versa.
The Logic: Lower unemployment → Labor shortages → Businesses raise wages to attract workers → Higher costs passed to consumers → Inflation rises

Policy Implication: Government faces a trade-off. Policies that reduce unemployment (stimulus, low interest rates) may increase inflation. Policies that fight inflation (raising interest rates) may increase unemployment.
bulletPoints: 1960s: Phillips Curve worked well—clear trade-off observed
1970s: Stagflation broke the curve—high inflation AND high unemployment
Today: Relationship is weaker but still influences Fed policy
layoutData.keyPoint: The Federal Reserve constantly balances inflation control vs. job creation—it's their dual mandate

content.question:
The Federal Reserve has to choose: Fight 6% inflation by raising interest rates (might cause recession and job losses) OR keep rates low to protect jobs (inflation stays high, eroding purchasing power).

If you were Fed Chair, which would you prioritize and why? Who gets hurt either way?

scenario.name: The 1970s: When Everything Went Wrong
paragraphs: The Setup: In 1973, OPEC (Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries) placed an oil embargo on the U.S., causing oil prices to quadruple. This created massive cost-push inflation.

The Problem: Normally, high inflation coincides with low unemployment (Phillips Curve). But in the 1970s, the U.S. experienced BOTH high inflation (12%+) AND high unemployment (9%+). This combination is called stagflation.
Why It's Terrible: Government couldn't win. Stimulus to reduce unemployment would worsen inflation. Raising interest rates to fight inflation would increase unemployment. The economy was trapped.

outcomes.label: 1970s Stagflation
outcomes.detail: Lasted for most of the decade

outcomes.label: Fed Response (1979-1982)
outcomes.detail: Caused severe recession but broke inflation

content.headerTitle: Real-World Example: How This Affects YOU

scenario.name: Sarah's Budget: 2019 vs. 2024
paragraphs: Sarah is 25 years old, working as a teacher with the same salary in 2019 and 2024 (no raise). Let's see how inflation affected her budget:
2019 Monthly Budget: Rent: $900, Groceries: $300, Gas: $120, Car insurance: $150, Utilities: $100. Total: $1,570
2024 Monthly Budget: Rent: $1,200 (+33%), Groceries: $420 (+40%), Gas: $180 (+50%), Car insurance: $180 (+20%), Utilities: $130 (+30%). Total: $2,110
Impact: Sarah needs an extra $540/month ($6,480/year) just to maintain the same lifestyle. Her purchasing power declined 34% in 5 years!

outcomes.label: 2024 Discretionary Income
outcomes.detail: Going into debt without a raise

content.question:
Your generation faces higher costs for housing, education, and healthcare than previous generations—yet entry-level wages haven't kept pace with inflation.

How does understanding inflation and unemployment change the way you think about career planning, salary negotiation, and financial decisions? What strategies can you use to protect yourself?

leftColumn.title: What Doesn't Work
items: Keeping cash under your mattress (loses 3%+ value per year)
Accepting jobs without negotiating COLA (cost-of-living adjustments)
Fixed-rate savings accounts with returns below inflation
Ignoring price increases and maintaining spending habits

rightColumn.title: What Works
items: Invest in assets that grow faster than inflation (stocks, real estate)
Choose careers with wage growth potential
Monitor CPI and adjust budget accordingly
Build income streams that scale with inflation

questions.question: Explain the difference between demand-pull, cost-push, and monetary inflation. Give an example of each.
Why is frictional unemployment considered healthy while cyclical unemployment is dangerous?
What is the Phillips Curve trade-off? Why did stagflation in the 1970s challenge this relationship?
How does inflation affect your purchasing power? Use the 1970 vs. 2024 comparison to explain.

takeaways.title: Inflation reduces purchasing power
takeaways.description: Your dollar buys less over time—3% inflation means $100 loses $3 of purchasing power annually

takeaways.title: Three causes of inflation
takeaways.description: Demand-pull (too much demand), cost-push (rising production costs), monetary (too much money printed)

takeaways.title: Three types of unemployment
takeaways.description: Frictional (temporary job searching), structural (skills mismatch), cyclical (recession-driven)

takeaways.title: Phillips Curve trade-off
takeaways.description: Historically, inflation and unemployment move in opposite directions—policy must balance both

takeaways.title: Protect yourself strategically
takeaways.description: Negotiate raises matching inflation, invest in assets that outpace inflation, choose careers with wage growth

main.title: Tomorrow's Learning Lab
main.description: Use the Inflation Impact Calculator to see how inflation affects YOUR budget and plan protection strategies

steps: Apply inflation rates to see 5-year and 10-year projections
Calculate the salary increases needed to maintain purchasing power
Create a personal inflation protection plan

content.tagline: "Understanding inflation and unemployment helps you protect your purchasing power and make smarter career decisions"
